paragraphs.text: © 2024 Agronomy Australia Conference, 21-24 October 2024, Albany, Australia. https://agronomyconference.com/ 1 Management strategies to reduce the risk of pre-harvest sprouting and low falling number in wheat. Jeremy Curry1,2 1 Dept. of Primary Industries and Regional Development, Esperance, WA 6450, Jeremy.curry@dpird.wa.gov.au 2 The University of Western Australia, Crawley, WA 6009. Abstract Pre-harvest sprouting (PHS) is a grain quality issue that occurs when grains germinate on the mother plant, adversely affecting the end-use quality and value of the grain upon harvest. Recent research conducted in Western Australia has observed that earlier maturing crops (resulting from early sowing dates and quick developing varieties) show increased pre-harvest sprouting incidence. Sequential field sampling has demonstrated that detrimental levels of PHS are occurring during the maturation drying period and well before harvest maturity (12-14% moisture content) has been reached. In these scenarios, even optimal harvest timing cannot prevent pre-harvest sprouting. As a result, proactive management of pre-harvest sprouting, particularly through appropriate cultivar choice (pre-harvest sprouting risk and maturity length) aligned correctly with sowing date are crucial in preventing its incidence. Keywords Pre-harvest sprouting, falling number, wheat, maturation, germination. Introduction Pre-harvest sprouting is a grain quality constraint to wheat production that occurs when grains germinate on the mother plant, adversely affecting the end-use quality and value of the grain upon harvest. The risk of this precocious germination changes throughout seed development, maturation and after-ripening and is influenced by complex interactions between genetic and environmental factors. Australian cultivars typically exhibit low levels of grain dormancy, a key trait for pre-harvest sprouting resistance by which germination is delayed or inhibited for a temporary period after maturation. As a result, wet conditions following grain maturation can lead to significant pre-harvest sprouting incidence in some seasons, such as 2022 where approximately 40% of wheat grown in New South Wales was downgraded to feed wheat (Black et al., 2023). In the wheat growing areas of Western Australia, which typically exhibit climates with progressively drier conditions through spring and summer, earlier maturing crops (through sowing date and cultivar maturity length) have been linked to increased pre-harvest sprouting incidence (Curry, 2022). These crops are exhibiting pre-harvest sprouting (through falling number testing and visual assessment) from soon after mass maturity (maximum grain dry weight, approximately 40 % moisture content) and well before drying to harvest maturity (approximately 12 % moisture content). Historically, timely harvest and variety choice have been the primary tactics to reduce the incidence of pre-harvest sprouting (Curry & Nicol, 2022), so these early reductions in falling number have major implications for the management of pre-harvest sprouting. Methods In 2022, two trials were established near Esperance (south-east coast of Western Australia). Both trials comprised of twenty-four wheat cultivars sown at four sowing dates in small plots of 8 m length by 6 rows (25.7 cm row spacing on 1.85 m plot centres). At the Gibson site, three replicates of each cultivar were randomised within each of the four sowing date (time of sowing, TOS) blocks, with each TOS sown into adequate moisture on 27 April (TOS1), 11 May (TOS2), 24 May (TOS3) and 07 June (TOS4). The Grass Patch site consisted of main plots of time of sowing in a randomised block design, with cultivars as sub plots. Outside of the main design, an additional six cultivars were randomised within the outside (buffer) runs of each sowing date replicate. Plots were sown into moisture on 11 April (TOS1), 29 April (TOS2), 16 May (TOS3) and 31 May (TOS4). Trials were managed to limit the influence of abiotic or biotic stress, with herbicides, fungicides, insecticides and fertiliser applied as per best practice. Plots were assessed for date of spike emergence, mid-anthesis, and mass maturity (indicated by loss of colour at the peduncle at 90% of stems). Four varieties, DS Pascal , Eagle Rock , RockStar and Scepter , from the first and third sowing dates from the Gibson trial and the second and third sowing dates from the Grass Patch buffers were selected for weekly sampling. A final sample of all plots at each site was taken from the Grass Patch trial (main plots) on 01 December, and from Gibson on 13 December. These grain samples were then stored for testing of falling number.© 2024 Agronomy Australia Conference, 21-24 October 2024, Albany, Australia. https://agronomyconference.com/ 2 Weekly sampling consisted of cutting approximately 1 m of crop row at two positions per plot from near mass maturity onwards, with samples cut at least 20 cm below the spike. Within six hours of sampling, grains from ten random spikes per sample were gently dissected, with three grains from each spike placed into duplicate petri dishes lined with two layers of filter paper, and two grains per spike placed into an aluminium tray. To each petri dish (containing 30 grains each), 6 ml of water was added, and dishes were placed above a pool of water in a sealed plastic box at 20 °C, and germination (% of grains with protrusion of the radicle through the pericarp) was assessed after 72 hours. The aluminium trays (containing twenty grains) were immediately weighed, dried at 70 °C for 48 hours, and reweighed, with moisture content (%) calculated on a wet weight basis. The remaining spikes were dried (28 °C, low humidity), threshed, and the grain stored for falling number testing. All grain samples to be tested for falling number were stored at 20 °C until January, milled, left exposed to equilibrate to ambient moisture, and then tested for falling number (7 g of flour adjusted to 14 % moisture content based on the average moisture content of each sample lot). Figure 1: (a) Grain moisture (%, wet weight basis, squares), germination (% after 72 hours, circles), and (b) falling number (seconds, triangles) of RockStar (orange) and Scepter (grey) from the first (dark) and third (light) sowing dates at Gibson in 2022, plotted against date of sampling (calendar date). Black bars indicate daily rainfall (mm recorded to 9am). Minimum falling number for milling grades (300 seconds) is indicated by the solid line. Results Falling number declined soon after maturation. At the first sowing date (TOS1) from the Gibson trial, Scepter and RockStar reached mass maturity around 28 October. The falling number of Scepter and RockStar are shown in Figure 1b. Despite having a falling number in excess of 400 seconds on 18 October, the falling number of RockStar declined to 288 seconds by 25 October, and to 70 seconds by 02 November. Scepter also showed a decline in falling number, although to a lesser extent. These values were relatively unchanged through the end of November, with Scepter declining slightly further (to 162 seconds) at the final sampling date of 13 December. The moderately dormant wheat, DS Pascal , and the PHS resistant line, Eagle Rock , maintained FN above 300 seconds at all sampling dates except for Eagle Rock at 13 December (282 seconds, data not shown). a) 0 10 20 30 40 50 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100%
Rainfall (mm) Grain moisture (%) Germination (72 hrs, %) b) 0 10 20 30 40 50 0 100 200 300 400 500

Rainfall (mm) Falling Number (seconds)© 2024 Agronomy Australia Conference, 21-24 October 2024, Albany, Australia. https://agronomyconference.com/ 3 At the third sowing date (TOS3) at Gibson, Scepter and RockStar reached mass maturity around 18 November. Although this also coincided with rainy weather, the effects on falling number were less significant, with Scepter maintaining a falling number in excess of 390 seconds through the whole sampling period. RockStar declined to 281 seconds by 23 November, with little change through the rest of the sampling period. DS Pascal and Eagle Rock had a falling number in excess of 400 seconds at all sampling times (data not shown). Table 1: Moisture content (%, wet weight basis), and germination (%, 72 hours germination in 1.5ml or 6ml water) of grains of RockStar and Scepter sampled at six different harvest dates from third sowing date at Gibson in 2022. Cultivar Measurement 09 Nov 15 Nov 23 Nov 29 Nov 06 Dec 13 Dec RockStar Moisture content 45 % 40 % 20 % 13 % 25 % 12 %

Pre-harvest sprouting occurred concurrently with declining grain dormancy and moisture. Wheat grains generally gain the capacity to germinate around mass maturity, although grain dormancy can inhibit germination in these grains until a period of after-ripening has occurred. The duration of dormancy differs between varieties and also within seeds of the same population. The decline in falling number of RockStar and Scepter occurred during the period in which their germination was limited due to dormancy (Figure 1a). On 25 October, by which time RockStar ’s falling number had declined below 300 seconds, both varieties only had around 30 % of grains germinate within 72 hours in controlled conditions. This rose to around 60 % by 02 November and above 90 % by 09 November, indicating a rapid decline in any grain dormancy. For comparison, the moderately dormant, DS Pascal , still showed just 54 % germination (72hrs, 6ml water) on 13 December (data not shown). The rapid decline in grain dormancy soon after mass maturity occurred simultaneously with declining grain moisture during the maturation drying phase of grain development. The cool and wet maturation conditions for Gibson TOS1 resulted in a prolonged drying phase, with moisture content not declining to below 20 % until 10 November (Figure 1a). Given inadequate moisture content is the limiting factor for germination of mature grains, it appears that grain moisture content may influence the risk of germination. Grains from the third sowing date at Gibson were tested for germination under adequate (6 ml) and limited water (1.5 ml) availability to determine the impact of elevated moisture content (Table 1). Although germination under adequate water availability showed a consistent increase as maturation and after-ripening progressed. under limited water availability (1.5 ml water), moisture content seemed to influence germination capability, with grains of higher (i.e. greater than 20 %) moisture content (e.g. 15 November) showing a greater germination than grains dried to near harvest maturity (12 % moisture content, e.g. 29 November). Cultivar choice and sowing date are key to limiting pre-harvest sprouting risk. In Western Australia’s wheat growing regions where environmental conditions typically become warmer and drier during spring and summer, cool and wet maturation conditions are more likely to occur earlier in spring. Therefore, sowing date and variety choice combinations that lead to earlier maturation are generally likely to increase risk of pre-harvest sprouting due to higher rainfall near maturity and a prolonged period of elevated gran moisture. Wheat growers are encouraged, when sowing early, to sow slower maturing varieties as these will be less exposed to precociously early maturation (which is also associated with asynchronous maturation). These strategies are also likely to increase yield by allowing adequate growth during important development stages such as tillering and the critical phase from stem elongation to flowering. When growers are sowing early, particular consideration should be given to the sprouting risk of their variety (Curry & Nicol, 2022). Adequate variety choice can limit risk, even if maturation coincides with wet conditions (see Figure 2). This was evident at Grass Patch in 2022, despite the cooler and wetter conditions occurring midway through the maturation window and so in some cases, varieties had a higher falling number at earlier sowing dates despite their prolonged after-ripening and exposure to rainfall. This supports the idea that elevated moisture associated with recent maturation exacerbates the incidence of pre-harvest sprouting from© 2024 Agronomy Australia Conference, 21-24 October 2024, Albany, Australia. https://agronomyconference.com/ 4 rainfall events. Despite this, varieties with adequate pre-harvest sprouting resistance maintained a falling number above 300 seconds. Figure 2: Final falling number of plots from (a) Gibson, sampled on 13 December, and (b) Grass Patch, sampled on 01 December, plotted against the date of mass maturity for twenty-four wheat cultivars at four sowing dates at each trial. Each data point represents the mean of three field replicates. Shape and colour of symbols indicate Falling Number Index (FNI) ratings from highest pre-harvest sprouting resistance (9) to lowest (1). Black bars indicate daily rainfall (mm to 9 am). Minimum falling number for milling grades (300 seconds) indicated by solid line. Sting , a cultivar of intermediate pre-harvest sprouting susceptibility, is circled in red to highlight the changes in falling number across the four sowing dates at each trial. Conclusion Maturation under cool and wet conditions has been associated with the increased incidence of pre-harvest sprouting, with detrimental decreases in falling number occurring from soon after physiological maturity and well before harvest maturity. Although measurable levels of grain dormancy are still evident at this point, the state of elevated grain moisture appears to increase the successful germination of grains under limited water potential (such as is the case for pre-harvest sprouting under field conditions). In response to this increased risk, precociously early maturation that coincides with typically cooler and wetter climatic conditions should be avoided through appropriate sowing dates and cultivar (maturity type) choice. Cultivar pre-harvest sprouting resistance is also key to reducing risk of pre-harvest sprouting associated downgrades, particularly in high-risk situations (such as crops sown early in the high rainfall zone).
